reasoning: The crowd forecast is ~0.30, but there are strong reasons to be more skeptical. First, the Biden administration just finalized a massive update to AI export controls in January 2025 (covering advanced chips and >10^26 FLOP model weights), leaving less immediate necessity for another major update within the same calendar year. Second, the Jan 2025 rules use dynamic performance metrics (for chips) and very high compute thresholds (for weights), effectively future-proofing them against 2025's expected AI advancements. Third, the transition to the Trump administration will likely cause delays in drafting complex new interagency rules, and Trump's explicit campaign promise to repeal Biden's AI Executive Order casts serious doubt on the survival of the previously proposed cloud computing (IaaS) KYC rules. While aggressive China hawks might push to close the cloud loophole, the requirement that the rule 'must apply broadly' and have an effective date in 2025 creates a tight bottleneck for a new administration navigating conflicting pressures from hawkish national security factions and anti-regulatory tech supporters.
